details: Long-term zinc supplementation (>40mg/day) can induce copper deficiency by competing for absorption. This can lead to anemia and neurological issues.
recommendation: If taking zinc long-term, supplement with copper (ratio of ~15:1 zinc:copper) or take copper separately.
timing_advice: Take zinc and copper at different times of day (4+ hours apart)

summary: Zinc and iron compete for absorption
details: High doses of zinc can interfere with iron absorption, and vice versa. Both use the same transport mechanisms.
recommendation: Take zinc and iron supplements at different times.

details: Calcium can reduce iron absorption by up to 50%. This is one of the most significant mineral interactions.
recommendation: Take iron supplements away from calcium-rich foods or supplements.

summary: High calcium can impair magnesium absorption
details: Very high calcium intake can reduce magnesium absorption. Ideal ratio is approximately 2:1 calcium to magnesium.
recommendation: Maintain balanced calcium:magnesium ratio. Consider splitting doses.
timing_advice: Can take together in moderate doses, or separate by 2 hours for high doses
sources: Journal of the American College of Nutrition

details: High-dose calcium supplements can interfere with zinc absorption.
recommendation: If taking both, separate timing or ensure adequate zinc intake.
timing_advice: Separate by 2 hours if taking high doses

summary: High-dose magnesium may slightly reduce zinc absorption
details: Very high magnesium doses (>142mg) may modestly reduce zinc absorption. Normal supplemental doses are fine together.
recommendation: Generally fine to take together at normal doses.
timing_advice: Can take together; separate only if taking very high doses
sources: Journal of the American College of Nutrition

summary: Vitamin D and K2 work synergistically
details: Vitamin D increases calcium absorption, while K2 directs calcium to bones and away from arteries. Together they optimize bone health and reduce cardiovascular calcification risk.
recommendation: Take D3 with K2, especially at higher vitamin D doses (>2000 IU).
timing_advice: Take together, ideally with a fat-containing meal

details: Vitamin D is essential for proper calcium absorption in the gut. Without adequate D, only 10-15% of dietary calcium is absorbed.
recommendation: Ensure adequate vitamin D status when supplementing calcium.

summary: Vitamin C dramatically enhances iron absorption
details: Vitamin C can increase non-heme iron absorption by 2-3x by converting iron to a more absorbable form and counteracting absorption inhibitors.
recommendation: Take iron with vitamin C for maximum absorption.

summary: High-dose vitamin C may reduce B12 absorption
details: Very high doses of vitamin C (>1000mg) taken simultaneously may reduce B12 absorption.
recommendation: Separate high-dose vitamin C from B12 supplements.
timing_advice: Separate by 2+ hours if taking high-dose vitamin C
sources: Journal of the American Dietetic Association

summary: High-dose vitamin E may interfere with vitamin K
details: Very high doses of vitamin E (>800 IU) can interfere with vitamin K's blood clotting function.
recommendation: Avoid very high vitamin E doses, especially if on blood thinners.

summary: Tyrosine and 5-HTP compete for brain uptake
details: Both amino acids use the same transporter to cross the blood-brain barrier. Taking together may reduce effectiveness of both.
recommendation: Take at different times of day for best effect.
timing_advice: Separate by 4+ hours; tyrosine in morning, 5-HTP in evening

summary: Both increase serotonin - risk of excess
details: Tryptophan and 5-HTP both increase serotonin levels. Taking together significantly increases risk of serotonin syndrome.
recommendation: Do not combine - choose one or the other.

summary: BCAAs may reduce tyrosine brain uptake
details: BCAAs compete with tyrosine and tryptophan for brain uptake. High BCAA doses may reduce catecholamine synthesis.
recommendation: If using tyrosine for cognitive effects, separate from BCAAs.

summary: Ashwagandha may increase thyroid hormone levels
details: Ashwagandha can stimulate thyroid function. For those on thyroid medication, this could cause hyperthyroidism.
recommendation: Consult doctor before combining. May need medication adjustment.
sources: Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine

details: Ashwagandha has GABAergic activity and may enhance the effects of sedatives, benzodiazepines, or sleep medications.
recommendation: Use caution, start with lower doses of both.

details: St. John's Wort increases serotonin levels. Combined with SSRIs, this can cause dangerous serotonin syndrome.
recommendation: Do NOT combine with antidepressants without medical supervision.

details: St. John's Wort induces liver enzymes that break down hormonal contraceptives faster, reducing their effectiveness.
recommendation: Do not use St. John's Wort if relying on hormonal birth control.

details: Ginkgo has blood-thinning properties. Combined with anticoagulants (warfarin, aspirin), it significantly increases bleeding risk.
recommendation: Avoid ginkgo if on blood thinners, or consult doctor.

details: Curcumin has very poor bioavailability. Piperine (black pepper extract) inhibits its metabolism, dramatically increasing absorption.
recommendation: Always take curcumin with piperine/black pepper or choose a bioavailable form.

details: Curcumin is fat-soluble. Taking with omega-3 or other fats significantly improves absorption.
recommendation: Take curcumin with fats like omega-3 or with meals.

details: Statin medications block the same pathway that produces CoQ10, leading to depletion. This may contribute to muscle pain side effects.
recommendation: Consider CoQ10 supplementation if taking statins.
timing_advice: Can take together; CoQ10 with fatty meal for absorption
sources: Journal of the American College of Cardiology

summary: Magnesium needed for vitamin D activation
details: Magnesium is required for vitamin D metabolism. Low magnesium can impair vitamin D effectiveness.
recommendation: Ensure adequate magnesium when supplementing vitamin D.
sources: Journal of the American Osteopathic Association

details: Vitamin C helps regenerate quercetin after it acts as an antioxidant, extending its effectiveness.
recommendation: Take quercetin with vitamin C for enhanced antioxidant effect.

details: Quercetin inhibits enzymes that metabolize resveratrol, potentially increasing its levels and effects.
recommendation: Consider combining for enhanced longevity benefits.

details: Magnesium supports GABA activity and muscle relaxation, while melatonin regulates sleep timing. Together they address different aspects of sleep.
recommendation: Safe and effective combination for sleep support.
sources: Journal of Research in Medical Sciences

details: Melatonin may have mild anticoagulant effects. Use caution if on blood thinners.
recommendation: Inform doctor if taking both; monitor for bleeding.

summary: Both support sleep through different mechanisms
details: Glycine lowers core body temperature and supports inhibitory neurotransmission. Magnesium supports GABA and muscle relaxation.
recommendation: Excellent combination for sleep quality.

details: L-theanine promotes calm focus and reduces caffeine's jittery side effects while preserving alertness benefits.
recommendation: Classic nootropic stack - take together for focused energy.
timing_advice: Take together; typical ratio is 2:1 theanine:caffeine

details: Fiber supplements can bind to medications and reduce their absorption. Affects many medications.
recommendation: Take medications 1-2 hours before or after fiber supplements.

details: High fiber intake, especially from supplements, can reduce absorption of zinc, iron, calcium, and magnesium.
recommendation: Take mineral supplements separately from fiber.

summary: Vitamin K reverses warfarin's blood-thinning effect
details: Warfarin works by inhibiting vitamin K-dependent clotting factors. Supplementing vitamin K directly counteracts this mechanism, potentially causing dangerous blood clots. Even dietary vitamin K changes can destabilize INR (International Normalized Ratio).
recommendation: Do NOT supplement vitamin K while on warfarin without medical supervision. Maintain consistent vitamin K intake from food. Inform your doctor of any diet changes.

details: MK-4 and MK-7 forms of vitamin K2 can interfere with warfarin. MK-7 has a longer half-life (~3 days) and may cause more sustained INR changes than K1. Even low doses can affect clotting.
recommendation: Avoid vitamin K2 supplements on warfarin. If prescribed, requires frequent INR monitoring.

summary: Fish oil may increase bleeding risk with warfarin
details: High-dose omega-3 fatty acids (>3g/day EPA+DHA) have antiplatelet effects and may potentiate warfarin's anticoagulation, increasing bleeding risk. Mechanism involves reduced thromboxane A2 synthesis and altered platelet membrane fluidity.
recommendation: Inform doctor if taking fish oil with warfarin. May need INR monitoring. Doses under 1g/day generally safe.
timing_advice: Monitor for bleeding signs; bruising, bloody nose, dark stools

summary: High-dose vitamin E increases warfarin bleeding risk
details: Vitamin E (>400 IU/day) inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting and enhances warfarin's effect. Can increase INR and bleeding risk. Mixed tocopherols may have stronger effect than alpha alone.
recommendation: Avoid high-dose vitamin E on warfarin. If needed, use under 200 IU/day with monitoring.

details: Garlic inhibits platelet aggregation through multiple mechanisms including inhibition of thromboxane synthesis. Combined with warfarin, significantly increases bleeding risk. Dietary garlic in normal amounts is generally safe.
recommendation: Avoid garlic supplements on warfarin. Inform doctor. Culinary use is acceptable.
timing_advice: Discontinue garlic supplements 7-10 days before surgery

details: Ginger inhibits thromboxane synthetase and may potentiate warfarin. Clinical significance is moderate - less concerning than garlic or vitamin E, but still warrants monitoring.
recommendation: Use ginger cautiously with warfarin. Small culinary amounts are fine.

details: Both ginkgo and aspirin inhibit platelet aggregation through different mechanisms. Ginkgo inhibits platelet-activating factor (PAF), while aspirin inhibits COX-1. Combined effect significantly increases bleeding risk, including intracranial bleeding.
recommendation: Avoid ginkgo if taking aspirin regularly. If combining, inform all doctors.
timing_advice: Stop ginkgo 2 weeks before any surgery

summary: Fish oil adds to aspirin's antiplatelet effect
details: Both reduce platelet aggregation. Fish oil's effect on bleeding is additive with aspirin. Generally safe in cardiac patients under medical supervision, but increases minor bleeding.
recommendation: Combination often prescribed for heart health but monitor for excessive bruising.
timing_advice: Watch for nosebleeds, easy bruising, prolonged cuts
sources: Journal of the American College of Cardiology

summary: Vitamin E enhances aspirin's antiplatelet effect
details: High-dose vitamin E (>400 IU) adds to aspirin's bleeding risk through inhibition of vitamin K-dependent clotting. Combination increases GI bleeding risk.
recommendation: Use lower vitamin E doses if on daily aspirin. Inform doctor.
timing_advice: Monitor for GI discomfort, dark stools

details: Nattokinase is a fibrinolytic enzyme that directly breaks down fibrin and inhibits platelet aggregation. Combined with any anticoagulant (warfarin, aspirin, DOACs), dramatically increases bleeding risk.
recommendation: Do NOT take nattokinase with any blood thinner. Consult doctor before use.
timing_advice: Avoid combination entirely; stop 2 weeks before surgery

summary: Dong quai significantly increases warfarin effect
details: Dong quai contains coumarins that have direct anticoagulant activity. Combined with warfarin, INR can increase dramatically (case reports of INR >10). Very dangerous interaction.
recommendation: Absolutely avoid dong quai while on warfarin.

details: Dan Shen (Salvia miltiorrhiza) inhibits CYP450 enzymes that metabolize warfarin and has intrinsic anticoagulant properties. Can dramatically increase INR and bleeding.
recommendation: Do not combine with warfarin. Common in Traditional Chinese Medicine formulas.

details: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril, ramipril) reduce potassium excretion. Adding potassium supplements can cause life-threatening hyperkalemia (high potassium), leading to fatal cardiac arrhythmias. Symptoms include muscle weakness, paralysis, heart palpitations.
recommendation: Do NOT supplement potassium while on ACE inhibitors without doctor approval and monitoring.
timing_advice: Monitor potassium levels regularly if combining

details: ARBs (losartan, valsartan, olmesartan) also reduce potassium excretion like ACE inhibitors. Same dangerous hyperkalemia risk with potassium supplements.
recommendation: Avoid potassium supplements on ARBs without medical supervision.
timing_advice: Regular potassium blood tests if supplementing
sources: Journal of the American Society of Nephrology

summary: Licorice raises blood pressure, opposing medication
details: Glycyrrhizin in licorice causes sodium retention and potassium loss (pseudoaldosteronism), raising blood pressure. Can counteract all BP medications. As little as 50g/day of licorice candy (if containing real licorice root) can cause hypertension.
recommendation: Avoid licorice supplements and excessive licorice candy on BP medications.
timing_advice: Effect persists 2 weeks after stopping licorice

summary: Magnesium may enhance BP lowering effect
details: Magnesium acts as a natural calcium channel blocker. High-dose supplementation with medications like amlodipine, diltiazem may cause excessive blood pressure lowering, dizziness, or fatigue.
recommendation: Start magnesium at lower doses and monitor BP if on CCBs.
timing_advice: Monitor blood pressure when starting magnesium

summary: CoQ10 may enhance BP medication effects
details: CoQ10 has modest blood pressure-lowering effects (average 11/7 mmHg reduction). This can add to BP medication effects, potentially allowing lower medication doses.
recommendation: Monitor BP. May be beneficial - discuss with doctor about medication adjustment.
timing_advice: Generally safe; monitor BP when starting

summary: Hawthorn adds to BP lowering effect
details: Hawthorn (Crataegus) has cardiovascular effects including vasodilation and mild ACE inhibition. Can enhance effects of BP medications, causing hypotension.
recommendation: Use cautiously with BP meds. Start low and monitor blood pressure.

details: Grapefruit inhibits CYP3A4 enzyme in gut, preventing first-pass metabolism. Can increase blood levels of felodipine by 300%, amlodipine by 50%. Risk of severe hypotension, edema, and arrhythmias. Effect lasts 24+ hours.
recommendation: Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice with CCBs. One glass can cause interaction.
timing_advice: Avoid entirely - timing separation doesn't help

details: Chromium picolinate enhances insulin action by improving insulin receptor sensitivity. Combined with insulin, may cause hypoglycemia (low blood sugar). Particularly risky with high-dose chromium (>1000 mcg).
recommendation: Monitor blood sugar closely. May need insulin dose reduction.
timing_advice: Check blood sugar more frequently when starting chromium

summary: Chromium adds to metformin's glucose-lowering effect
details: Both chromium and metformin improve insulin sensitivity. Combination may cause hypoglycemia, especially if reducing carbohydrate intake simultaneously.
recommendation: Monitor blood sugar. Generally safe but watch for hypoglycemia symptoms.
timing_advice: Monitor glucose levels, especially before meals

summary: ALA lowers blood sugar - may enhance diabetes meds
details: Alpha-lipoic acid improves glucose uptake and insulin sensitivity. Studies show 600-1800mg/day can lower fasting glucose by 10-30%. Adds to diabetes medication effects.
recommendation: Monitor blood sugar closely. May need medication dose adjustment.
timing_advice: Check glucose levels frequently when starting ALA

details: Berberine activates AMPK (same pathway as metformin) and lowers blood glucose as effectively as metformin in some studies. Combining may cause hypoglycemia and potentially increases lactic acidosis risk.
recommendation: Do not combine without medical supervision. One or the other, not both.
timing_advice: Monitor glucose and watch for GI side effects

details: Bitter melon (Momordica charantia) contains compounds that mimic insulin and stimulate glucose uptake. Can add to diabetes medication effects.
recommendation: Monitor blood sugar if using bitter melon with diabetes medications.

details: Fenugreek seeds contain galactomannan fiber that slows carbohydrate absorption. Can lower post-meal glucose spikes and may enhance diabetes medication effects.
recommendation: Start with low doses. Monitor blood sugar.
timing_advice: Take 30 minutes before meals with water

summary: Gymnema reduces sugar absorption and cravings
details: Gymnema sylvestre blocks sugar receptors on taste buds and intestinal wall, reducing sugar absorption. May enhance diabetes medication glucose-lowering.
recommendation: Monitor blood sugar. May help reduce medication needs over time.
timing_advice: Take before meals to reduce sugar absorption
sources: Journal of Clinical Biochemistry and Nutrition

details: Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, pantoprazole) and H2 blockers reduce stomach acid needed for calcium absorption. Long-term PPI use linked to increased fracture risk. Calcium citrate absorbs better than carbonate in low-acid environment.
recommendation: Take calcium separately from antacids. Use calcium citrate if on long-term PPIs.
timing_advice: Take calcium 2 hours before or after antacids

details: Iron requires stomach acid for conversion to absorbable Fe2+ form. PPIs and H2 blockers can reduce iron absorption by 65-75%, potentially causing iron deficiency.
recommendation: Take iron supplements separately from antacids. Consider heme iron sources.
timing_advice: Take iron 2+ hours before antacids, ideally with vitamin C

details: Stomach acid is required to release B12 from food proteins. Long-term PPI use (>2 years) increases B12 deficiency risk by 65%. Can cause anemia, neuropathy, cognitive decline. Sublingual B12 bypasses this issue.
recommendation: Monitor B12 levels if on long-term PPIs. Consider sublingual or injection B12.
timing_advice: Use sublingual B12 which doesn't require stomach acid

details: Long-term PPI use associated with hypomagnesemia (low magnesium). FDA issued warning in 2011. Can cause muscle cramps, arrhythmias, seizures. Risk increases with duration.
recommendation: Monitor magnesium levels on long-term PPIs. Supplement if low.
timing_advice: Take magnesium supplements at different time than PPI

details: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (ibuprofen, naproxen) can increase renal magnesium excretion and reduce intestinal absorption with long-term use.
recommendation: Consider magnesium supplementation if using NSAIDs regularly.

details: Benadryl (diphenhydramine) is an anticholinergic sedative. Combined with melatonin, may cause excessive drowsiness, next-day grogginess, cognitive impairment.
recommendation: Choose one or the other. If combining, use minimal doses.
timing_advice: Be aware of enhanced sedation; don't drive

summary: NAC protects against acetaminophen liver damage
details: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is the antidote for acetaminophen overdose. It replenishes glutathione, which protects the liver from NAPQI (toxic acetaminophen metabolite). Prophylactic NAC may offer protection with regular acetaminophen use.
recommendation: NAC may be protective for regular acetaminophen users. Excellent safety profile.
timing_advice: Can take together; NAC is hepatoprotective

details: 5-HTP is a direct serotonin precursor. Combined with SSRIs (which prevent serotonin reuptake), can cause dangerous serotonin excess. Serotonin syndrome symptoms: agitation, hyperthermia, tremor, rigid muscles, potentially fatal.
recommendation: Do NOT combine 5-HTP with any SSRI, SNRI, or MAOI. Life-threatening interaction.

summary: SAM-e with SSRIs: Serotonin syndrome risk
details: SAM-e increases serotonin synthesis and activity. Combined with SSRIs, increases serotonin syndrome risk. Some psychiatrists do combine carefully under supervision, but not without monitoring.
recommendation: Do not combine without psychiatric supervision. Risk of serotonin syndrome.

summary: Melatonin may enhance SSRI side effects
details: Some SSRIs (especially fluvoxamine) inhibit CYP1A2, which metabolizes melatonin. This can increase melatonin blood levels, causing excessive sedation.
recommendation: Use lower melatonin doses (0.3-0.5mg) with SSRIs. Monitor for oversedation.

summary: Ginseng may cause hypertensive crisis with MAOIs
details: Ginseng may have MAO-inhibiting properties. Combined with MAOI antidepressants, can cause dangerous blood pressure elevation, hypertensive crisis, and potentially serotonin syndrome.
recommendation: Avoid ginseng entirely while on MAOIs.

details: Both kava and benzodiazepines enhance GABA activity. Combined, can cause profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma. One case report of patient becoming comatose.
recommendation: Do NOT combine kava with benzodiazepines or other sedatives.

summary: Valerian adds to sedative medication effects
details: Valerian has GABAergic activity similar to benzodiazepines. Can potentiate effects of sedatives, sleep medications, and antihistamines.
recommendation: Use caution combining valerian with any sedative. Lower doses of both.

summary: Tyrosine can cause hypertensive crisis with MAOIs
details: Tyrosine is a precursor to dopamine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine. MAOIs prevent breakdown of these neurotransmitters. Combining can cause dangerous catecholamine surge with severe hypertension, potentially stroke.
recommendation: Do NOT take tyrosine with MAOIs.

summary: Rhodiola may have mild MAOI effects
details: Rhodiola rosea may have mild MAO-inhibiting properties. Combined with SSRIs, theoretical risk of serotonin syndrome, though clinical reports are limited.
recommendation: Use cautiously with SSRIs. Start with low doses and monitor mood.
timing_advice: Start with low doses; watch for agitation, anxiety

details: Calcium binds to levothyroxine (Synthroid) in the gut, reducing absorption by up to 60%. This can lead to subtherapeutic thyroid levels and hypothyroid symptoms.
recommendation: Take thyroid medication at least 4 hours before or after calcium supplements.
timing_advice: Separate by 4+ hours; thyroid med first thing in morning

details: Iron forms insoluble complexes with levothyroxine, reducing absorption significantly. Can cause treatment failure and persistent hypothyroid symptoms.
recommendation: Take thyroid medication 4+ hours before iron supplements.
timing_advice: Thyroid med in morning, iron in evening

summary: Magnesium may reduce thyroid med absorption
details: Magnesium can form complexes with levothyroxine, though effect is less pronounced than calcium or iron. Still recommended to separate doses.
recommendation: Take thyroid medication at least 4 hours before magnesium.

summary: Soy may interfere with thyroid medication
details: Soy isoflavones may interfere with levothyroxine absorption and possibly thyroid function itself. Effect varies between individuals.
recommendation: Maintain consistent soy intake. Don't take soy supplements same time as thyroid med.
timing_advice: Separate soy supplements from thyroid medication by 4+ hours

details: Both excess and deficient iodine affect thyroid function. High-dose iodine supplements can cause thyroid dysfunction in susceptible individuals, including those on thyroid medication. Kelp supplements often contain unpredictable iodine amounts.
recommendation: Avoid high-dose iodine or kelp supplements on thyroid medication. Discuss iodine status with your doctor.
timing_advice: Avoid iodine supplements unless medically supervised

details: Selenium is essential for deiodinase enzymes that convert T4 to active T3. Adequate selenium may improve thyroid medication effectiveness, especially in autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's).
recommendation: 200 mcg selenium may support thyroid function. Don't exceed this dose.
timing_advice: Can take separately from thyroid medication

summary: St. John's Wort can cause organ rejection
details: St. John's Wort strongly induces CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, dramatically reducing cyclosporine blood levels (up to 50% reduction). Has caused documented cases of acute heart transplant rejection. Life-threatening interaction.
recommendation: ABSOLUTELY AVOID St. John's Wort if on cyclosporine or any immunosuppressant.
timing_advice: Do not take at any time while on immunosuppressants

details: Echinacea stimulates immune function, potentially counteracting immunosuppressant medications. Risk of organ rejection or autoimmune flares.
recommendation: Avoid echinacea if on immunosuppressants for transplant or autoimmune disease.

details: Astragalus (Huang Qi) enhances immune function and may counteract immunosuppressive therapy. May increase risk of rejection or autoimmune flares.
recommendation: Avoid astragalus on immunosuppressants.

details: Quercetin increases cellular uptake of zinc by acting as a zinc ionophore, potentially enhancing zinc's antiviral and immune-supporting effects.
recommendation: Effective combination for immune support.

details: Vitamin A improves iron absorption and mobilization from stores. Deficiency of either impairs the function of the other. Particularly important in developing countries.
recommendation: Combining may improve iron status, especially in deficiency.

details: Vitamin D is fat-soluble and absorbs better with dietary fat. Taking with omega-3 fish oil provides the fat needed for optimal vitamin D uptake.
recommendation: Take vitamin D with omega-3 or fatty meal.
sources: Journal of the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics

details: Piperine from black pepper inhibits glucuronidation and enhances bioavailability of many supplements including ashwagandha. May increase effectiveness.
recommendation: Can take ashwagandha with piperine for enhanced absorption.

details: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) facilitates magnesium transport into cells. Combined they may be more effective than magnesium alone, especially for PMS and stress.
recommendation: Consider combination for enhanced magnesium benefits.

summary: Zinc needed for vitamin A metabolism
details: Zinc is required for retinol-binding protein synthesis and vitamin A metabolism. Zinc deficiency impairs vitamin A function even with adequate vitamin A intake.
recommendation: Ensure adequate zinc when supplementing vitamin A.

details: Boron may reduce urinary excretion of vitamin D and extend its half-life. Also supports bone health independently through calcium and magnesium metabolism.
recommendation: 3mg boron may support vitamin D status and bone health.

summary: Long-term PPI use reduces B12 absorption
details: Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, esomeprazole, etc.) suppress stomach acid needed to release B12 from food proteins. Use >2 years is associated with a ~65% increase in B12 deficiency risk.
recommendation: If on long-term PPIs, supplement with methylcobalamin (1000mcg) or sublingual B12 which bypasses the need for gastric acid.
timing_advice: Take B12 supplement separately; form matters more than timing

details: The FDA issued a safety warning in 2011: PPI use >1 year is associated with low serum magnesium, sometimes severe enough to require hospitalization. Mechanism involves reduced intestinal absorption.
recommendation: Monitor magnesium levels annually on long-term PPIs. Supplement with 200-400mg magnesium glycinate if levels are low.
timing_advice: Take magnesium 2+ hours away from PPI dose

details: Calcium carbonate requires stomach acid to dissolve. Long-term PPI use is associated with reduced bone density and increased fracture risk, partly via impaired calcium absorption.
recommendation: On PPIs, switch from calcium carbonate to calcium citrate — citrate does not require gastric acid for absorption.
timing_advice: Take calcium with meals regardless of PPI timing

details: Gastric acid is required to reduce ferric iron to the absorbable ferrous form. PPI use can worsen iron-deficiency anemia, especially in people with marginal iron status.
recommendation: If iron-deficient on PPIs, take iron bisglycinate (requires less acid) with vitamin C to enhance absorption. Consider checking ferritin.
timing_advice: Take iron 2+ hours away from PPI with 500mg vitamin C

summary: Antibiotics kill probiotic bacteria if taken together
details: Oral antibiotics will kill live probiotic organisms if taken at the same time, making the probiotic useless. Spacing them out preserves both.
recommendation: Continue probiotics during an antibiotic course — they reduce antibiotic-associated diarrhea. Take the probiotic 2-3 hours after the antibiotic dose.
timing_advice: Separate by at least 2-3 hours; continue for 2+ weeks after finishing antibiotics

details: Ciprofloxacin, doxycycline, minocycline, and other fluoroquinolones and tetracyclines form insoluble complexes with iron, dramatically reducing absorption of both the drug and the mineral.
recommendation: Take iron 2 hours before or 6 hours after these antibiotics to avoid reducing the antibiotic's effectiveness.
timing_advice: Separate by at least 2 hours before or 6 hours after antibiotic

summary: Caffeine may blunt some creatine effects
details: An older study suggested caffeine could interfere with creatine's performance benefits, though more recent research is mixed. Any effect is likely modest and dose-dependent. Both are widely co-supplemented in pre-workouts without obvious problems.
recommendation: If you want to be conservative, take creatine post-workout and caffeine pre-workout. For most people co-timing is fine.

summary: Creatine and beta-alanine stack well for power + endurance
details: Creatine improves short-burst power output (≤10 sec); beta-alanine buffers muscle acidity and extends high-intensity endurance (1-4 min). Combined supplementation outperforms either alone for most athletes.
recommendation: Standard doses: 3-5g creatine monohydrate daily, 3.2-6.4g beta-alanine daily (split into 2-3 doses to reduce paresthesia).
sources: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition

summary: HMB + creatine synergy for strength and lean mass
details: A 2001 study found combined HMB (3g/day) and creatine (10g/day) produced greater gains in strength and fat-free mass than either alone.
recommendation: If already on creatine, adding 3g HMB daily may further enhance strength gains — especially during caloric deficit or deload periods.

summary: B12 and folate work together in methylation
details: B12 and folate are both required for DNA methylation and red blood cell formation. Supplementing folate alone can mask B12 deficiency by correcting anemia while allowing neurological damage to progress — always co-supplement.
recommendation: When taking folate (especially >400mcg), always include 500-1000mcg B12. Prefer methylfolate + methylcobalamin for MTHFR variants.

summary: Phytates in fiber reduce non-heme iron absorption
details: Whole grains, legumes, and high-fiber plant foods contain phytates and oxalates that bind non-heme iron, reducing absorption by up to 50%. Vegetarians and vegans need ~1.8× more iron as a result.
recommendation: Take iron supplements between meals, or with vitamin C which overrides the phytate-binding effect.
timing_advice: Take iron at least 1-2 hours away from high-fiber meals

summary: Vitamin E protects omega-3 fats from oxidation
details: Omega-3 fatty acids are highly prone to peroxidation both on the shelf and in the body. Most quality fish oil products already include mixed tocopherols as an antioxidant for this reason.
recommendation: Choose a fish oil that lists mixed tocopherols on the label. No need to separately megadose vitamin E.

details: Ashwagandha has mild serotonergic and GABAergic activity. Adding it to an SSRI is not known to cause serotonin syndrome but may potentiate sedation, dizziness, or mood changes, especially during dose changes.
recommendation: If on an SSRI, introduce ashwagandha at a low dose (300mg) and monitor. Discuss with your prescriber before adding.
timing_advice: If combined, take at different times of day to separate peak effects

summary: Vitamin C is required for collagen synthesis
details: Vitamin C is a cofactor for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase, the enzymes that cross-link collagen fibers. Without adequate vitamin C the body cannot effectively incorporate collagen amino acids into new tissue.
recommendation: Take 50-500mg vitamin C with collagen peptides (10-20g) for best use.

summary: NAC is the rate-limiting precursor to glutathione
details: N-acetylcysteine supplies the cysteine needed to synthesize glutathione, the body's primary intracellular antioxidant. Oral glutathione has poor bioavailability, so raising glutathione via NAC is usually more effective.
recommendation: 600-1200mg NAC daily is typically more effective than oral glutathione for raising cellular glutathione levels.
timing_advice: Take NAC on an empty stomach

summary: Taurine and magnesium support sleep and cardiovascular calm
details: Taurine modulates GABA activity and calcium signaling; magnesium supports NMDA receptor regulation and muscle relaxation. The combination is a well-established nighttime stack for stress and sleep support.
recommendation: 500-2000mg taurine + 200-400mg magnesium glycinate, 30-60 min before bed.

details: Like statins, some beta blockers (propranolol, metoprolol) can inhibit CoQ10 biosynthesis. Not as well-documented as statin depletion, but supplementation is often considered for long-term users with fatigue.
recommendation: 100-200mg ubiquinol daily is reasonable for long-term beta blocker users — especially if experiencing exercise intolerance.
timing_advice: Take with a fat-containing meal for absorption
